content.text: is the practice of defining cloud resources in text files that are version-controlled, reviewed, and deployed through automated pipelines — the same engineering discipline applied to software code. Instead of clicking through the AWS Console to create a VPC or launch an EC2 instance, you write a configuration file that describes the desired state, and a tool provisions the resources to match that state.

IaC addresses a fundamental problem with manual infrastructure management: it does not scale, it is not reproducible, and it leaves no reliable audit trail. A console configuration performed by a different engineer three months ago is nearly impossible to reconstruct exactly. A Terraform or CloudFormation definition from three months ago, with its full Git history, is perfectly reproducible. This is why IaC is the baseline expectation for any team running production workloads on AWS.

attrs.alt: IaC tools and deployment workflow diagram
attrs.title: Infrastructure as Code — Tools and Workflow

content.text: The practical benefits of IaC emerge from the combination of

: the same configuration file, applied to different AWS accounts, produces identical infrastructure. This is how organizations maintain true parity between dev, staging, and production — not by hoping engineers followed the same manual steps.

: infrastructure definitions live in Git alongside application code. Pull requests enforce review gates before infrastructure changes reach production. Rollback is `git revert` and a pipeline run, not a manual reconstruction.
: every infrastructure change is a commit with an author, timestamp, and message. Compliance auditors can review infrastructure history without parsing CloudTrail API logs.

: when someone makes an emergency console change, the next IaC plan shows the drift. The team explicitly decides whether to codify the change (add it to the IaC definition) or revert it (apply the existing IaC). Nothing is silently lost.

: IaC makes it possible to run cost estimation before deployment. Tools like `infracost` integrate with Terraform plans to show the monthly cost of proposed infrastructure changes before they are applied.
is AWS's native IaC service, free to use (you pay only for the resources it provisions). Templates are written in YAML or JSON and describe
— logical groups of AWS resources that are created, updated, and deleted together. CloudFormation maintains the
of a stack and handles dependencies automatically: if a Lambda function depends on an IAM role and an S3 bucket, CloudFormation creates them in the correct order.

The core CloudFormation template structure has five main sections: `Parameters` (runtime inputs), `Resources` (the AWS resources to create — required), `Outputs` (values to export, such as a load balancer DNS name), `Mappings` (static key-value tables for lookup), and `Conditions` (logic for conditional resource creation). The `Resources` section is the only required section.

# cloudformation/vpc.yaml — a simple VPC with two public subnets
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: VPC with public subnets for the payments service

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, staging, production]
    Default: dev
  VpcCidr:
    Type: String
    Default: 10.0.0.0/16

Resources:
  VPC:
    Type: AWS::EC2::VPC
    Properties:
      CidrBlock: !Ref VpcCidr
      EnableDnsHostnames: true
      EnableDnsSupport: true
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: !Sub "${Environment}-vpc"
        - Key: Environment
          Value: !Ref Environment

  PublicSubnet1:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Subnet
    Properties:
      VpcId: !Ref VPC
      CidrBlock: 10.0.1.0/24
      AvailabilityZone: !Select [0, !GetAZs ""]
      MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: !Sub "${Environment}-public-1"

  PublicSubnet2:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Subnet
    Properties:
      VpcId: !Ref VPC
      CidrBlock: 10.0.2.0/24
      AvailabilityZone: !Select [1, !GetAZs ""]
      MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true

  InternetGateway:
    Type: AWS::EC2::InternetGateway

  VPCGatewayAttachment:
    Type: AWS::EC2::VPCGatewayAttachment
    Properties:
      VpcId: !Ref VPC
      InternetGatewayId: !Ref InternetGateway

Outputs:
  VpcId:
    Value: !Ref VPC
    Export:
      Name: !Sub "${Environment}-VpcId"   # cross-stack reference
  PublicSubnet1Id:
    Value: !Ref PublicSubnet1
    Export:
      Name: !Sub "${Environment}-PublicSubnet1Id"

CloudFormation stacks are managed via the console, CLI, or CI/CD pipelines. The `aws cloudformation deploy` command is the most pipeline-friendly: it creates the stack if it does not exist, updates it if it does, and returns a non-zero exit code on failure. The `--no-fail-on-empty-changeset` flag prevents pipeline failure when there are no changes to apply (common in idempotent deployments).

# Deploy (create or update) a CloudFormation stack
aws cloudformation deploy   --template-file cloudformation/vpc.yaml   --stack-name payments-vpc-production   --parameter-overrides Environment=production VpcCidr=10.1.0.0/16   --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM   --no-fail-on-empty-changeset   --region us-east-1

# Preview changes without applying them (change set)
aws cloudformation create-change-set   --stack-name payments-vpc-production   --template-body file://cloudformation/vpc.yaml   --change-set-name preview-update-cidr   --parameters ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=production

aws cloudformation describe-change-set   --stack-name payments-vpc-production   --change-set-name preview-update-cidr   --query "Changes[*].{Action:ResourceChange.Action,Resource:ResourceChange.LogicalResourceId,Type:ResourceChange.ResourceType}"

# List stack outputs
aws cloudformation describe-stacks   --stack-name payments-vpc-production   --query "Stacks[0].Outputs[*].{Key:OutputKey,Value:OutputValue}"   --output table

# Delete a stack and all its resources
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name payments-vpc-dev
lets you define CloudFormation infrastructure using familiar programming languages: Python, TypeScript, Java, C#, and Go. CDK code is

into CloudFormation templates (the `cdk synth` command), which are then deployed via the CloudFormation service. This means CDK uses the same deployment engine as hand-written CloudFormation — it just replaces YAML/JSON authoring with real programming language constructs: loops, conditionals, functions, and type safety.

(collections of stacks). AWS provides a library of pre-built constructs (`aws-cdk-lib`) for every AWS service, ranging from low-level wrappers (L1 constructs that map directly to CloudFormation resource types) to high-level abstractions (L3 constructs like `aws_ecs_patterns.ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService` that provision an entire load-balanced Fargate service with sensible defaults in a few lines).

# cdk_app/vpc_stack.py
from aws_cdk import (
    Stack,
    aws_ec2 as ec2,
    aws_rds as rds,
    CfnOutput,
)
from constructs import Construct

class VpcStack(Stack):
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, construct_id: str, env_name: str, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(scope, construct_id, **kwargs)

        # L2 construct — high-level VPC with sensible defaults
        self.vpc = ec2.Vpc(self, "AppVpc",
            vpc_name=f"{env_name}-vpc",
            ip_addresses=ec2.IpAddresses.cidr("10.0.0.0/16"),
            max_azs=2,
            nat_gateways=1,  # 0 for dev to save cost
            subnet_configuration=[
                ec2.SubnetConfiguration(name="Public",  subnet_type=ec2.SubnetType.PUBLIC,  cidr_mask=24),
                ec2.SubnetConfiguration(name="Private", subnet_type=ec2.SubnetType.PRIVATE_WITH_EGRESS, cidr_mask=24),
            ]
        )

        # RDS PostgreSQL with automatic security group wiring
        db = rds.DatabaseInstance(self, "AppDatabase",
            engine=rds.DatabaseInstanceEngine.postgres(
                version=rds.PostgresEngineVersion.VER_15_4
            ),
            instance_type=ec2.InstanceType.of(ec2.InstanceClass.T3, ec2.InstanceSize.MICRO),
            vpc=self.vpc,
            vpc_subnets=ec2.SubnetSelection(subnet_type=ec2.SubnetType.PRIVATE_WITH_EGRESS),
            multi_az=(env_name == "production"),
            deletion_protection=(env_name == "production"),
        )

        CfnOutput(self, "VpcId", value=self.vpc.vpc_id,
                  export_name=f"{env_name}-VpcId")

# app.py
import aws_cdk as cdk
from cdk_app.vpc_stack import VpcStack

app = cdk.App()
VpcStack(app, "DevVpcStack",   env_name="dev",        env=cdk.Environment(region="us-east-1"))
VpcStack(app, "ProdVpcStack",  env_name="production", env=cdk.Environment(region="us-east-1"))
app.synth()

# CDK workflow
npm install -g aws-cdk      # install CDK CLI
cdk bootstrap               # one-time setup: creates S3 bucket + IAM roles in account/region

cdk synth                   # synthesize to CloudFormation — shows the YAML output
cdk diff                    # preview changes against deployed stacks
cdk deploy DevVpcStack      # deploy one stack
cdk deploy --all            # deploy all stacks in the app
cdk destroy DevVpcStack     # delete stack and all resources
— an open-source fork) is the most widely adopted multi-cloud IaC tool. It uses
— a purpose-built, human-readable declarative language. Terraform maintains a
that records the current known state of managed resources. The state file is the source of truth for what Terraform has created — it maps your configuration to actual cloud resource IDs.

The core Terraform workflow is `init → plan → apply → destroy`. `terraform plan` compares the desired state (your `.tf` files) against the current state (the state file) and shows a diff of what will be created, modified, or destroyed. This preview — the
— is the most important artifact to review before applying changes. In production pipelines, the plan output is typically posted as a PR comment for human review before the apply runs.

# Terraform workflow
terraform init                    # download providers, configure backend
terraform plan -out=tfplan        # preview changes, save plan to file
terraform apply tfplan            # apply the saved plan (no re-confirmation)
terraform show                    # show current state in human-readable form
terraform state list              # list all resources in state
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-0abc123  # import existing resource into state
terraform destroy                 # destroy all managed resources
Choosing Between CloudFormation, CDK, and Terraform
Low for YAML; high for CFN quirks
Low for HCL; medium for state ops
Teams that prefer real programming languages
For teams starting fresh on AWS,

offers the best combination of AWS-native integration (no state files, built-in rollback, free) and developer ergonomics (real programming languages with type safety and IDE support). For teams already using Terraform across multiple cloud providers, adding Terraform's AWS provider is the consistent choice.
alone (without CDK) is most appropriate when you need compliance-friendly declarative templates that non-engineers can read and review, or when you are deploying via AWS Service Catalog.

IaC reaches its full value when integrated into CI/CD pipelines that automatically plan and apply infrastructure changes on every code merge. The standard pipeline pattern for Terraform or CloudFormation is:
(the diff is posted as a PR comment for human review) and
merge to main triggers the apply
. This gate ensures no infrastructure change reaches production without code review.

for this. The CodeBuild project runs `terraform plan` or `cdk diff` on PR events and posts the output to the PR. On merge, a separate CodeBuild project runs `terraform apply` or `cdk deploy`. Credentials are provided by a CodeBuild service role — no long-lived access keys are needed in the pipeline.

# buildspec.yml for a Terraform CI pipeline stage
version: 0.2
phases:
  install:
    commands:
      - wget -q https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/1.7.0/terraform_1.7.0_linux_amd64.zip
      - unzip terraform_1.7.0_linux_amd64.zip -d /usr/local/bin/
  pre_build:
    commands:
      - terraform -version
      - cd infra/
      - terraform init -backend-config="bucket=${TF_STATE_BUCKET}"
  build:
    commands:
      - |
        if [ "$ACTION" = "plan" ]; then
          terraform plan -out=tfplan -no-color 2>&1 | tee plan-output.txt
          # Post plan output to PR (via GitHub API or CodeCommit)
        elif [ "$ACTION" = "apply" ]; then
          terraform apply -auto-approve -no-color
        fi
  post_build:
    commands:
      - echo "Terraform $ACTION complete with exit code $CODEBUILD_BUILD_SUCCEEDING"
Storing sensitive values in IaC files

: never put database passwords, API keys, or secrets directly in `.tf` or `.yaml` files. Use AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store and reference the secret ARN in IaC; let the application retrieve the actual value at runtime.
State file not in remote backend

: a local `terraform.tfstate` file means only one person can apply changes safely, and it is lost if the machine is destroyed. Always configure an S3 backend with DynamoDB locking for any team or production use.

: if engineers are allowed to make console changes in production, IaC definitions will drift. Either enforce IaC-only access via SCPs that deny manual resource changes, or run `terraform plan` / `cdk diff` on a schedule and alert on drift.
Deleting a resource without understanding dependencies

: in Terraform, removing a resource from `.tf` files and running `apply` destroys it. RDS deletions that bypass the final snapshot trigger `deletion_protection` errors — intentionally. Always review plan output that shows 'destroy' actions carefully.

: splitting infrastructure into multiple small stacks (networking, security, application) reduces blast radius on failures and speeds up applies. A stack with 500 resources takes much longer to update than five stacks with 100 resources each.

are the primary mechanism for reusable infrastructure patterns. A module is a directory of `.tf` files with defined inputs (variables) and outputs. When a module is called from another configuration, inputs are passed as arguments and outputs are referenced like any other Terraform value. This encapsulates complex multi-resource patterns — a 'VPC with public and private subnets, NAT gateway, and route tables' — into a single call with a few parameters.
Modules can be sourced from the

(registry.terraform.io), a Git repository, or a local directory path. AWS publishes official modules for common patterns such as `terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws`, `terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws`, and `terraform-aws-modules/rds/aws`. These modules are tested, actively maintained, and encode AWS best practices — using them is faster than writing the equivalent configuration from scratch and reduces the chance of missing a security-relevant default.

# Using the official AWS VPC module from the Terraform Registry
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"

  name = "${var.environment}-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway     = true
  single_nat_gateway     = (var.environment != "production")  # save cost in non-prod
  enable_dns_hostnames   = true

  tags = {
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# Reference module outputs elsewhere in the same config
resource "aws_db_subnet_group" "main" {
  name       = "${var.environment}-db-subnet-group"
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnets   # output from the VPC module
}

# Or writing your own module (modules/rds/main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf)
module "payments_db" {
  source      = "./modules/rds"
  vpc_id      = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids  = module.vpc.private_subnets
  environment = var.environment
  db_name     = "payments"
}

IaC files define security-relevant configuration — IAM roles, security groups, encryption settings, public access flags. Scanning IaC before it is deployed catches security misconfigurations before they become running resources. This is significantly cheaper than detecting and remediating a live exposed S3 bucket or an overly permissive security group.

(open-source) are the two most common IaC security scanners for Terraform and CloudFormation. Checkov also supports CDK-synthesized CloudFormation output, Kubernetes manifests, and Docker files. Integrating a scanner into the CI pipeline makes security a gate: the plan cannot proceed if a scan finds high-severity violations. AWS's own
(`cfn-guard`) provides a policy-as-code engine for CloudFormation templates with custom rules written in a Guard DSL.

# Install and run Checkov on Terraform files
pip install checkov
checkov -d infra/                    # scan all .tf files in the infra directory
checkov -f infra/main.tf             # scan a single file
checkov -d infra/ --framework terraform --check CKV_AWS_79  # run a specific check

# Run Checkov on a CDK-synthesized CloudFormation template
cdk synth > cdk.out/MyStack.template.json
checkov -f cdk.out/MyStack.template.json --framework cloudformation

# Example Checkov output:
# Check: CKV_AWS_79: "Ensure Instance Metadata Service Version 1 is not enabled"
#   FAILED for resource: aws_instance.web
#   Guide: https://docs.bridgecrew.io/docs/bc_aws_general_31

# tfsec — fast scanner for Terraform
brew install tfsec        # macOS
tfsec infra/              # scan all Terraform files
tfsec infra/ --format json | jq '.results[] | select(.severity == "HIGH")'

# CloudFormation Guard — AWS-native policy validation
pip install cloudformation-guard
cfn-guard validate   --data cloudformation/vpc.yaml   --rules rules/security-baseline.guard

# Example rules/security-baseline.guard rule:
# rule vpc_flow_logs_required {
#   AWS::EC2::FlowLog exists
#   <<VPC Flow Logs must be enabled for all VPCs>>
# }

occurs when the actual state of a cloud resource diverges from the state described in IaC — typically because someone made a manual change via the console or CLI. Drift is the natural enemy of IaC: if engineers can bypass the IaC pipeline for 'quick' changes, those changes accumulate until the IaC definition no longer reflects reality, and the next deployment breaks or overwrites something unexpectedly.

feature: the `detect-stack-drift` API checks each resource in a stack against its current actual configuration and reports any properties that have changed. Terraform detects drift by running `terraform plan` — resources that were modified outside Terraform will show as changes in the plan even if the `.tf` files have not changed. The plan will show what Terraform will do to bring the resource back to the desired state.
The organizational response to drift depends on the severity:
the drift by applying the IaC (if the manual change was unauthorized or undocumented), or

the drift by updating the IaC to match the manual change and committing it. Either path requires an explicit decision. Some teams enforce IaC-only access in production via Service Control Policies that deny `ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress`, `rds:ModifyDBInstance`, and other commonly bypassed operations directly, making console changes impossible and forcing all changes through the IaC pipeline.

# CloudFormation drift detection
aws cloudformation detect-stack-drift --stack-name payments-vpc-production
# Returns: StackDriftDetectionId: ...

# Check drift detection status
aws cloudformation describe-stack-drift-detection-status   --stack-drift-detection-id abc123

# Get drifted resources once detection is complete
aws cloudformation describe-stack-resource-drifts   --stack-name payments-vpc-production   --stack-resource-drift-status-filters MODIFIED DELETED   --query "StackResourceDrifts[*].{Resource:LogicalResourceId,Status:StackResourceDriftStatus}"

# Terraform drift detection — run plan with no code changes
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode
# Exit 0: no changes (no drift)
# Exit 2: changes detected (drift exists)
# Exit 1: error

# Automate drift alerts — run in a cron pipeline
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=drift-check.tfplan 2>&1
EXIT=$?
if [ $EXIT -eq 2 ]; then
  echo "DRIFT DETECTED in production infrastructure"
  # Send alert to Slack / PagerDuty / JIRA
fi
IaC defines cloud infrastructure in version-controlled code files, enabling repeatability, auditability, and drift prevention that manual console management cannot provide.
AWS CloudFormation is AWS's native IaC engine — free, with automatic rollback, and no state file to manage. Templates are YAML or JSON.
AWS CDK compiles programming language code (Python, TypeScript, Java) into CloudFormation templates, adding type safety, loops, conditions, and reusable constructs.
Terraform uses HCL and a provider model that supports AWS, Azure, GCP, and hundreds of other services — the standard for multi-cloud organizations.
Store state remotely (S3 + DynamoDB for Terraform); never commit secrets to IaC files; always review plan output before applying — especially any 'destroy' actions.
IaC reaches full value in CI/CD pipelines: plan on pull requests for human review, apply on merge to main for automated deployment.
